online_resume_results.query: A column face has honeycombing and exposed aggregate after formwork removal.

online_top3.title: Concrete column honeycombing detected after form removal
action_checklist: Sound the affected area and carry out a depth assessment by engineer or competent inspector.
Verify reinforcement cover and determine whether structural repair is required.
Review pour sequence, vibration records, and formwork tightness for the affected lift.
Protect the area from impact and restrict loading until disposition is confirmed.
Prepare a repair method statement if patching, epoxy injection, or breakout and reinstatement is needed.
online_top3.escalation_rule: Escalate to structural engineer immediately if defect depth approaches reinforcement, cover is compromised, or multiple columns exhibit similar honeycombing.

online_top3.title: Concrete spalling at slab edge after formwork striking
action_checklist: Suspend further aggressive stripping or edge cleaning until the affected area is assessed.
Check whether reinforcement cover is exposed and whether repair depth is limited to surface concrete.
Review striking method, curing duration, and concrete strength test results for the pour.
Document the defect extent and protect the edge from impact or additional loading.
Complete approved repair and curing measures before reopening the slab edge for use.
online_top3.escalation_rule: Escalate to the engineer if reinforcement is exposed, if the spall extends beyond a localized patch, or if similar damage is found at multiple edges.

online_top3.title: Hairline crack observed near lift core wall opening
action_checklist: Measure and mark crack length, width, and orientation using a calibrated gauge.
Check whether the crack is live by monitoring for seasonal or load-related movement.
Review recent pours, stripping sequence, and temporary loading around the core wall.
Inspect for associated moisture ingress, spalling, or adjacent cracking patterns.
Escalate for structural review if the crack widens, propagates, or appears through-thickness.
online_top3.escalation_rule: Escalate to structural engineer if crack width exceeds project tolerance, shows displacement, or is accompanied by leakage or progressive propagation.

online_resume_results.scenario: Water ingress becomes electrical safety issue
online_resume_results.query: Water seepage is close to a temporary electrical riser in the basement after rain.

online_top3.title: Water ingress approaching temporary electrical riser
action_checklist: Isolate the affected temporary electrical circuit and verify de-energization by a competent person.
Keep personnel away from the wet area and establish a controlled exclusion zone.
Identify the water source and apply temporary diversion, containment, or pumping as needed.
Inspect the riser for ingress, damaged insulation, and elevated termination points.
Restore power only after the area is dry, the source is controlled, and electrical integrity is confirmed.
online_top3.escalation_rule: Escalate immediately to electrical supervisor and site safety if live equipment is threatened, if any arc/odor/heat is observed, or if isolation cannot be verified.

online_top3.title: Basement wall water ingress observed at construction joint
action_checklist: Mark and photograph the full extent of seepage and note elevation/chainage references.
Inspect the joint detailing, membrane continuity, and any penetrations or tie-hole repairs.
Check adjacent drainage, sump operation, and external water management conditions.
Implement temporary water control measures and isolate any affected electrical or finish-sensitive areas.
Raise a corrective action for waterproofing specialist review and retesting after repair.
online_top3.escalation_rule: Escalate immediately if leakage increases, reaches active electrical equipment, or shows signs of wall movement, cracking, or soil washout.

online_top3.title: Temporary works prop shows visible deformation
action_checklist: Immediately clear personnel from the supported area and prohibit loading on the temporary arrangement.
Inspect all related props, bracing, and bearing conditions for signs of instability or settlement.
Verify the approved temporary works design against actual loading and geometry.
Install additional supports only under the direction of the temporary works coordinator or engineer.
Record the defect and do not reinstate use until the system is recertified safe.
online_top3.escalation_rule: Escalate immediately to the temporary works coordinator, construction manager, and safety lead; treat as a potential collapse precursor.

initial_offline.answer:
This looks closest to [INC-002] Concrete column honeycombing detected after form removal (severity: high).

What I can ground from the local evidence: A reinforced concrete column shows honeycombing in the lower lift area following formwork removal. The defect is localized but extends over enough surface area to indicate poor consolidation or leakage during casting, requiring assessment of depth and impact on cover.

Likely cause to check: Probable inadequate vibration, congested reinforcement impeding flow, grout loss through formwork joints, or improper concrete placement technique.

Recommended next actions:
1. Sound the affected area and carry out a depth assessment by engineer or competent inspector
2. Verify reinforcement cover and determine whether structural repair is required
3. Review pour sequence, vibration records, and formwork tightness for the affected lift
4. Protect the area from impact and restrict loading until disposition is confirmed
5. Prepare a repair method statement if patching, epoxy injection, or breakout and reinstatement is needed

Escalate if: Escalate to structural engineer immediately if defect depth approaches reinforcement, cover is compromised, or multiple columns exhibit similar honeycombing.

Also compare against [INC-001], [INC-005] if the photo does not match the top case.

Grounding: answered from retrieved local evidence for [INC-002].

enriched_offline_after_sync.answer:
This looks closest to [INC-002] Concrete column honeycombing detected after form removal (severity: high).

What I can ground from the local evidence: A reinforced concrete column shows honeycombing in the lower lift area following formwork removal. The defect is localized but extends over enough surface area to indicate poor consolidation or leakage during casting, requiring assessment of depth and impact on cover.

Likely cause to check: Probable inadequate vibration, congested reinforcement impeding flow, grout loss through formwork joints, or improper concrete placement technique.

Recommended next actions:
1. Sound the affected area and carry out a depth assessment by engineer or competent inspector
2. Verify reinforcement cover and determine whether structural repair is required
3. Review pour sequence, vibration records, and formwork tightness for the affected lift
4. Protect the area from impact and restrict loading until disposition is confirmed
5. Prepare a repair method statement if patching, epoxy injection, or breakout and reinstatement is needed

Escalate if: Escalate to structural engineer immediately if defect depth approaches reinforcement, cover is compromised, or multiple columns exhibit similar honeycombing.

Also compare against [ONL-010], [INC-001] if the photo does not match the top case.

Grounding: answered from retrieved local evidence for [INC-002].

top3.title: Concrete spalling at slab edge after formwork striking
action_checklist: Suspend further aggressive stripping or edge cleaning until the affected area is assessed.
Check whether reinforcement cover is exposed and whether repair depth is limited to surface concrete.
Review striking method, curing duration, and concrete strength test results for the pour.
Document the defect extent and protect the edge from impact or additional loading.
Complete approved repair and curing measures before reopening the slab edge for use.
top3.escalation_rule: Escalate to the engineer if reinforcement is exposed, if the spall extends beyond a localized patch, or if similar damage is found at multiple edges.

top3.title: Hairline crack observed near lift core wall opening
action_checklist: Measure and mark crack length, width, and orientation using a calibrated gauge.
Check whether the crack is live by monitoring for seasonal or load-related movement.
Review recent pours, stripping sequence, and temporary loading around the core wall.
Inspect for associated moisture ingress, spalling, or adjacent cracking patterns.
Escalate for structural review if the crack widens, propagates, or appears through-thickness.
top3.escalation_rule: Escalate to structural engineer if crack width exceeds project tolerance, shows displacement, or is accompanied by leakage or progressive propagation.

initial_offline.answer:
This looks closest to [INC-001] Basement wall water ingress observed at construction joint (severity: high).

What I can ground from the local evidence: Active water ingress is visible at the basement wall joint, with staining extending below the suspect construction joint and moisture tracking toward the floor slab edge. No immediate structural distress is evident, but the leakage suggests a compromised waterproofing detail or joint seal failure.

Likely cause to check: Likely discontinuity in waterproofing membrane, inadequate joint preparation, or failed waterstop/sealant at a construction joint combined with hydrostatic pressure.

Recommended next actions:
1. Mark and photograph the full extent of seepage and note elevation/chainage references
2. Inspect the joint detailing, membrane continuity, and any penetrations or tie-hole repairs
3. Check adjacent drainage, sump operation, and external water management conditions
4. Implement temporary water control measures and isolate any affected electrical or finish-sensitive areas
5. Raise a corrective action for waterproofing specialist review and retesting after repair

Escalate if: Escalate immediately if leakage increases, reaches active electrical equipment, or shows signs of wall movement, cracking, or soil washout.

Also compare against [INC-005], [INC-002] if the photo does not match the top case.

Grounding: answered from retrieved local evidence for [INC-001].

enriched_offline_after_sync.answer:
This looks closest to [ONL-007] Water ingress approaching temporary electrical riser (severity: critical).

What I can ground from the local evidence: Water ingress is occurring close to a temporary electrical riser, creating an immediate risk of electrical shock, short circuit, and service interruption. The condition is time-sensitive because the seepage path may reach live distribution components if not isolated.

Likely cause to check: Likely failed waterproofing, rainwater intrusion, or uncontrolled surface drainage compounded by insufficient protection and routing of temporary electrical infrastructure.

Recommended next actions:
1. Isolate the affected temporary electrical circuit and verify de-energization by a competent person
2. Keep personnel away from the wet area and establish a controlled exclusion zone
3. Identify the water source and apply temporary diversion, containment, or pumping as needed
4. Inspect the riser for ingress, damaged insulation, and elevated termination points
5. Restore power only after the area is dry, the source is controlled, and electrical integrity is confirmed

Escalate if: Escalate immediately to electrical supervisor and site safety if live equipment is threatened, if any arc/odor/heat is observed, or if isolation cannot be verified.

Also compare against [INC-001], [ONL-010] if the photo does not match the top case.

Grounding: answered from retrieved local evidence for [ONL-007].

This looks closest to [INC-005] Unsafe open edge adjacent to scaffold access point (severity: critical).

What I can ground from the local evidence: An open edge is present immediately adjacent to scaffold access, creating a high-risk fall exposure for workers transitioning between the scaffold and the slab. The hazard is uncontrolled and requires immediate protection or access restriction.

Likely cause to check: Likely removal or omission of edge protection during sequencing, inadequate supervision, or incomplete scaffold-to-structure interface controls.

Recommended next actions:
1. Stop access to the affected route until edge protection is installed or alternative safe access is provided
2. Install compliant guardrails, toe boards, and controlled access measures at the perimeter
3. Inspect scaffold tie-ins, landing condition, and opening protections for related defects
4. Brief all nearby crews on the exclusion and safe access route
5. Record the hazard in the permit or daily safety log and verify closure before restart

Escalate if: Escalate immediately to site management and safety leadership; work in the area must remain stopped until the fall hazard is fully controlled.

Also compare against [INC-002], [INC-001] if the photo does not match the top case.

Install compliant guardrails, toe boards, and controlled access measures at the perimeter.
Inspect scaffold tie-ins, landing condition, and opening protections for related defects.
Brief all nearby crews on the exclusion and safe access route.
Record the hazard in the permit or daily safety log and verify closure before restart.
top3.escalation_rule: Escalate immediately to site management and safety leadership; work in the area must remain stopped until the fall hazard is fully controlled.

enriched_offline_after_sync.answer:
This looks closest to [ONL-011] Confined-space gas detector alarm triggered during drainage inspection (severity: critical).

What I can ground from the local evidence: A gas detector alarm activated during drainage inspection, indicating an unsafe atmosphere in or near the confined space. The condition may involve oxygen deficiency, toxic gas, or flammable gas, and no further entry should continue until the atmosphere is proven safe.

Likely cause to check: Likely accumulation of sewer gas, inadequate ventilation, residual biological decay, or disturbed sediment releasing hazardous vapors.

Recommended next actions:
1. Withdraw all personnel from the confined space area and prevent re-entry
2. Test atmosphere from outside the space using calibrated equipment at multiple levels
3. Implement forced ventilation only if approved and appropriate for the hazard identified
4. Confirm rescue arrangements, permit status, and competent supervision before any re-entry
5. Investigate source conditions and revise the confined-space entry controls accordingly

Escalate if: Escalate immediately to confined-space rescue resources and site management if anyone is inside the space, if gas readings are unstable, or if there is any sign of distress.

Also compare against [ONL-007], [ONL-010] if the photo does not match the top case.

Grounding: answered from retrieved local evidence for [ONL-011].

top3.title: Falling object exclusion zone breached beneath overhead work
action_checklist: Stop overhead work until the drop zone is fully re-established and enforced.
Remove unnecessary materials, tools, and loose items from elevated workfaces.
Rebrief all affected workers on exclusion limits and access permissions.
Assign a spotter or marshal to prevent unauthorized entry into the hazard area.
Review whether overhead work requires additional netting, tool lanyards, or rigid barricades.
top3.escalation_rule: Escalate to the area supervisor and HSE lead if repeated breaches occur, if overhead lifting is active, or if hard barricading is not feasible.

top3.title: Temporary works prop shows visible deformation
action_checklist: Immediately clear personnel from the supported area and prohibit loading on the temporary arrangement.
Inspect all related props, bracing, and bearing conditions for signs of instability or settlement.
Verify the approved temporary works design against actual loading and geometry.
Install additional supports only under the direction of the temporary works coordinator or engineer.
Record the defect and do not reinstate use until the system is recertified safe.
top3.escalation_rule: Escalate immediately to the temporary works coordinator, construction manager, and safety lead; treat as a potential collapse precursor.

phi4_role: Local CLIP embeds the worker text plus query image for retrieval.
Phi-4-mini-instruct is text-only; it is not the vector database and does not directly inspect the image.
Before sync it can only answer from the limited local evidence, so it should expose uncertainty and escalation.
After connectivity resumes and relevant online cases are cached, Phi-4-mini can draft richer offline answers from the updated local evidence.
Use Phi-4-multimodal-instruct if direct image+text generation is required on the SLM itself.

phi4_examples.answer:
This looks closest to [ONL-007] Water ingress approaching temporary electrical riser (severity: critical).

What I can ground from the local evidence: Water ingress is occurring close to a temporary electrical riser, creating an immediate risk of electrical shock, short circuit, and service interruption. The condition is time-sensitive because the seepage path may reach live distribution components if not isolated.

Likely cause to check: Likely failed waterproofing, rainwater intrusion, or uncontrolled surface drainage compounded by insufficient protection and routing of temporary electrical infrastructure.

Recommended next actions:
1. Isolate the affected temporary electrical circuit and verify de-energization by a competent person
2. Keep personnel away from the wet area and establish a controlled exclusion zone
3. Identify the water source and apply temporary diversion, containment, or pumping as needed
4. Inspect the riser for ingress, damaged insulation, and elevated termination points
5. Restore power only after the area is dry, the source is controlled, and electrical integrity is confirmed

Escalate if: Escalate immediately to electrical supervisor and site safety if live equipment is threatened, if any arc/odor/heat is observed, or if isolation cannot be verified.

Also compare against [INC-001], [ONL-010] if the photo does not match the top case.

Grounding: answered from retrieved local evidence for [ONL-007].

phi4_examples.answer:
This looks closest to [ONL-010] Temporary works prop shows visible deformation (severity: critical).

What I can ground from the local evidence: A temporary works prop is visibly deformed, indicating possible overload, instability, or foundation settlement. This is a critical condition because failure of temporary support could trigger partial collapse or sudden load redistribution.

Likely cause to check: Possible overload from premature loading, inadequate bracing, incorrect prop capacity selection, or base settlement on an insufficient bearing surface.

Recommended next actions:
1. Immediately clear personnel from the supported area and prohibit loading on the temporary arrangement
2. Inspect all related props, bracing, and bearing conditions for signs of instability or settlement
3. Verify the approved temporary works design against actual loading and geometry
4. Install additional supports only under the direction of the temporary works coordinator or engineer
5. Record the defect and do not reinstate use until the system is recertified safe

Escalate if: Escalate immediately to the temporary works coordinator, construction manager, and safety lead; treat as a potential collapse precursor.
